title: GPT-5 vs GPT-5 Mini: Compare Prices Cost Grid
description: Within-provider face-off: GPT-5 against GPT-5 mini in the Compare Prices grid, with the 4× price multiplier broken down.

sections.heading: Why a Side-by-Side Comparison of GPT-5 and GPT-5 mini Matters
sections.body: The [Compare Prices tool](/tools/compare-prices) is the fastest way to put a shortlist of LLMs in a single grid — input cost, output cost, context window, and quality score in stacked columns you can scan vertically. Provider dropdowns let you mix models across Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, DeepSeek, Mistral, and xAI without leaving the page. For GPT-5 vs GPT-5 mini, the side-by-side framing matters because both models sit near the same workload niche — one of you ships the wrong pick and the bill (or quality regression) is months of pain. GPT-5 runs $1.25 / $10.00 per 1M tokens with a 200K context and a blended quality score of 82. GPT-5 mini runs $0.300 / $2.40 per 1M with a 128K context and quality 70. Sticker prices don't tell the whole story — the Value column (quality ÷ input cost) gives GPT-5 a 65.6 and GPT-5 mini a 233.3, which is the number you actually want to optimize when shipping production traffic. Related reading: [quality per dollar LLM ranking 2026](/blog/quality-per-dollar-llm-ranking-2026), [LLM color-coded quality badges explained](/blog/llm-color-coded-quality-badges-explained), and [why the cheapest LLM isn't always the best value](/blog/why-cheapest-llm-isnt-always-best-value).

sections.heading: GPT-5 in the Compare Prices Grid
sections.body: In the [Compare Prices view](/tools/compare-prices), click the **OpenAI** dropdown and check **GPT-5**. The row shows input at $1.25/1M, output at $10.00/1M, 200K context, and the blended quality badge at 82. GPT-5 sits in TokenRate's **flagship** tier — flagship tier is for frontier-quality use cases where the per-token price is a rounding error against the value of the output. The output-to-input ratio of 8.0x is worth flagging because generation-heavy workloads (long summaries, code, structured output) compound that multiplier across every reply. For a single-shot classifier the input price dominates; for an agent generating ~10× the tokens it reads, you're effectively paying $10.00 per 1M.

sections.heading: GPT-5 mini in the Compare Prices Grid
sections.body: Add **GPT-5 mini** from the **OpenAI** dropdown. The grid lists input $0.300/1M, output $2.40/1M, 128K context, quality 70, tier **balanced**. balanced tier is the production-default zone — quality high enough for customer traffic, price low enough to scale. Compared to GPT-5, GPT-5 mini is cheaper on input (by 76%) and lower on quality (by 12 points). Context-window-wise, GPT-5 mini has a tighter window — relevant if you're feeding long documents.

sections.heading: Where GPT-5 Wins and Where GPT-5 mini Wins
sections.body: **GPT-5 mini wins on raw cost** ($0.300 vs $1.25 input — about 4.2× cheaper) — so it's the right pick for high-volume features where the model is fungible across the chosen tier. **GPT-5 wins on quality** (82 vs 70) — important when you're routing reasoning-heavy or accuracy-critical traffic. **GPT-5 mini wins on Value** (233.3 vs 65.6) — meaning per dollar of input you get more quality-adjusted output, which is what the [Value column](/blog/value-column-vs-tokens-per-dollar-metric) optimizes for. For long-context tasks, GPT-5's 200K window wins.

sections.heading: Decision Heuristics and What to Do Next
sections.body: Three heuristics: (1) if your monthly bill on the pricier option exceeds 4× your engineering team's comfort and the cheaper option's quality is within 5 points — ship the cheaper one and pocket the savings. (2) if the workload is reasoning-heavy or customer-facing premium, pay the quality premium even when the Value column says otherwise. (3) hedge: route 80–90% of traffic to the cheaper model and fall back to the pricier one for tail-quality cases. The fallback router pattern works because output-cost only matters when you actually call it. For the routing implementation, see [multi-model routing with quality scores](/blog/multi-model-routing-with-quality-scores). Both the price denominator (OpenRouter) and the quality numerator (Arena AI + Artificial Analysis) refresh hourly. So the comparison you screenshot Monday morning is still trustworthy at standup Tuesday morning — but you should re-run it before a quarterly model-routing review.

faq.question: How do I open the Compare Prices grid?
faq.answer: Two ways: click the 'Compare Prices' tab at the top of the calculator card on the home page, or navigate directly to /tools/compare-prices. The standalone page is also linked from the main navigation under 'Tools'.

faq.question: Can I share my comparison with teammates?
faq.answer: Yes — the page URL captures the current state. Send the link in Slack and your teammate sees the same grid. Useful for procurement and architecture-review meetings.

faq.question: Is the data live or cached?
faq.answer: Live from OpenRouter (prices) and a blended Arena AI + Artificial Analysis pipeline (quality), refreshed on a 60-minute incremental cache. So the grid is at most an hour stale.

faq.question: Where do I go after the grid to project monthly cost?
faq.answer: Once you've picked a winner, go to /tools/api-cost-estimator and plug in the model + your expected monthly token volume. The estimator does the per-1M math against your real workload mix.

ctaText: Run the comparison live at [/tools/compare-prices](/tools/compare-prices), then bookmark the URL for next month's price audit.
